chapters.title: Authenticity begins when the performance ends
chapters.text:
Millions feel lost and worn out at a certain point. Some struggle for years before realizing marriage no longer works; others force themselves to jobs they hate. People exist in such conditions for years, ignoring signals their inner self sends. Like an exhausted driver, they barely notice road signs. Most require a life-changing event—a huge roadblock—showing they move in the wrong direction.

For centuries people neglected true selves and sacrificed desires for the "greater good." They became "comfortable" for others, staying within commonly accepted norms and adjusting tastes and views to the masses.

No matter how many masks you wear, your inner self remains the same; you need bravery to uncover it.

Over years, people developed ego as a defense mechanism to accommodate reality. A child reprimanded for negative emotions learns to suppress them, piling up such "truths" until realizing others' worldviews should not define theirs.

Recognizing the wrong way directs you to the right path.

Understanding that you live in a fog of somebody else's perceptions clarifies vision. Ask who you are. Is this your desired life, or are you meeting expectations? Maybe you fear choosing yourself will deprive you of love. Whatever it is, being true to yourself means living fully, even if you lose something insignificant or false.

Prepare to meet your authentic self by shedding ego and unmasking core beliefs. Learn to grant yourself the love and validation you seek.

chapters.title: The trap of trying to be "good enough"
chapters.text:
According to Dr. Shefali, women still suffer from patriarchal, highly demanding cultural norms. While society expects men to do hard manual work, block emotions, and keep an alpha facade, women learn to remain between self-sacrifice and desperate need for validation.

A society where everyone wears a mask can never be healthy.

Since birth, every person faces pressure and expectations. Females are more assessed than seen. People comment on a girl's beauty or lack thereof, encouraging women to hide natural appearance behind makeup, diets, or surgery so society's appreciation matters more than their bodies.

Women often feel in a beauty contest, creating an inner void they fill with approval from men. Dynamics begin in childhood when a girl earns parents' validation by being a "good girl," prioritizing others' needs and complying. Some receive praise only when behaving as expected, forfeiting genuine selves. The "good girl" label may doom a woman for life as she demands similar approval from partners.

In a race for being "good enough," women sacrifice needs, desires, and beliefs, yet the goal is unachievable. "Good enough" means "perfect," and the notion is highly subjective. A woman adjusts behavior in contradictory ways to meet expectations, losing true self among masks while unworthiness remains.

Becoming authentic is the only way to long-lasting peace, yet it brings discomfort: telling truth, expressing emotions, prioritizing needs, setting boundaries.

Finding yourself involves external loss, but you may sacrifice more becoming "that woman" from magazine pages with mesmerizing curves, spotless career, and top homemaker skills. Striving to be "good enough" condemns you to competition bringing pain in exchange for love and validation.

Did you know? According to a Forbes article from May 2022, low self-esteem negatively affects professional advancement of four in five women.

chapters.title: Interrupt the script to restart your life
chapters.text:
Few realize how the unconscious rules life. They choose partners, react to events, face challenges, and manage conflicts as parents did or as learned in childhood.

Thought and behavioral patterns control immediate reaction and prevail in habits. A belief that you must earn love, approval, or attention as in childhood can motivate serving others in adulthood, depending on partners, or tolerating ill-treatment.

Most seek happiness externally: travel, new relationships, or working constantly to fill inner void. Inner issues need inner solutions. Talking to friends, watching movies, or spending time with loved ones distracts but is not a long-lasting remedy.

Becoming authentic implies shedding everything that supported false identity, even relationships. Some would rather drown in a sinking ship than rock it during turmoil; they play roles not to upset anyone and do not notice catastrophe approaching.

Relationships are a mirror reflecting your sense of self.

Radical awakening is becoming aware of masks used to escape pain and adjust to life. When you recognize patterns, destroy them by giving yourself what you lack: love, approval, attention, validation.

No one loses themselves to another. They come to the other already lost. ~ Dr. Shefali

Living with eyes open means living actively. Nothing happens to you; it is for you. Negative experiences change color when you realize your reaction made them painful. No situation is a dead-end when you prioritize yourself.

Mourning is essential to letting go.

Enhancing awareness is a habit you can start now. Analyze life and patterns; check emotions. Are you satisfied? Do you live as authentic self, or is something controlling you? What do you gain letting ego act? What masks do you wear and why? Ask whenever you choose or face challenge. Notice patterns to transform life and become genuine self.

chapters.title: How the ego manages—and limits—your life
chapters.text:
Ego is the inner "manager" you unconsciously create; it tells you what to do so fears do not come true. Fear of not being needed makes ego value people's time and serve their needs above yours.

Ego protected you and covered wounds for years, yet you cannot live with it forever—it is not authentic self. It does not heal or fill voids with love; it hides and numbs them so you function in society.

People use different facades by life path. The Giver is trendy among women fearing abandonment or indifference from family and partner. Codependent, unconfident people use this facade to earn validation, readily sacrificing time, effort, money, or life to serve others for something in return.

If your ego were a remote control, your fears would push its buttons.

The Giver's ego plays martyr or victim—suffering for others' good or feeling so sorry for themselves they never take responsibility. They exhaust themselves with work and domestic duties, refuse help believing everything is "on their shoulders," then blame others for hardships and emotions.

Ego is a Band-Aid, not medicine.

Ego never does anything without profit. Givers receive compassion, attention, or pity. They grant themselves medals for work done for others, rarely reaching goals or dreams, living family's or friends' lives, waiting for reciprocity.

The Giver's mind plays savior or empath who cannot feel happy without guilt, say no, or set boundaries. A savior mindset may choose drug-abusing or violent partners to "save" for life—choosing a project more than a person, rarely acknowledging the choice, as victims rarely feel responsible.

chapters.title: Three ego types and the pain of unmasking
chapters.text:
The Giver is one of three principal ego groups, associated with generosity, compassion, and support. The other two include:

• The Controllers

While Givers strive to be "good ones," Controllers seek dominance and success, detesting failure. Inner void pushes harder work, often leading to exhaustion from constant competition. They take charge of work, family, finances, and community. Inner critic never sleeps; they are never satisfied with achievements.

A Controller parent may sacrifice everything to become best mother or father, even against child's will. They work twice as hard for best schools yet rarely respect child's boundaries or choices—winning unrealistic parent-of-the-year contests rather than making children happy.

People from this group become passive-aggressive tyrants, combining Giver and Controller. Women accumulate frustration saying yes to every request—cleaning, cooking, homework help—then lash out manipulating through guilt when exhausted because no one helps. Shame follows emotional expression; they compensate by catering again. The circle never ends.

Tearing ego away is painful because, with every passing year, the mask grows further into the skin.

• The Takers

This ego type uses others as servants or tools for goals. Entitlement and superiority make them demand everything be done because they "deserve" it or it is "birthright." Takers rarely employ power beyond bossing others around. Self-worth depends on validation, yet unlike Givers and Controllers, they expect it without earning—acting helpless, childish, irresponsible for attention, angry and anxious when denied.

chapters.title: Mass culture and the stories we never questioned
chapters.text:
After meeting ego and detecting its grip, loosen it by acting differently. Further inward travel encounters false beliefs.

Cultural lies travel generation to generation, becoming part of nations. Mass culture conditions people to buy expensive Valentine's presents. Thousands waste hours in queues and salary chunks on roses withering in three days. Cultural masterminds rule brains of masses; most never realize they live in someone else's matrix or question guidelines.

We don't live a life; we live a pattern. ~ Dr. Shefali

People live by beliefs more than facts. Emotions make preconceptions powerful, making fact and fiction hard to differentiate. Most never examine origins of stories they believe, becoming puppets while mass culture pulls strings.

Immense changes take time but start in one person's head.

Break limits set by societal lies by "waking up." Detect and question how you live. Why are you doing what you do—is it because someone told you, or family tradition? Put beliefs and values on trial. Cultivate awareness daily, even in mundane chores. You may use laundry products because of ads, not quality. Awareness does not require rebelling against mass production, but when you see patterns, you cut apron strings and choose what life and laundry products must be.

Seeking external validation forces people to become mass culture followers until it seems "normal." Living by others' beliefs deprives energy and inner power. Life cannot be unique and happy until you realize you walk a well-worn path. Live in "what is," not "what if," and choose thoughts as you select clothes—or you end up looking like everyone else.

chapters.title: Daily practices for mindful authenticity
chapters.text:
Radical awakening shifts how you see world and self, yet it will not last unless supported daily. Past patterns are like addictions; their hold goes unnoticed. Choosing different reaction in partner fights takes courage and consciousness, yet success shows authority and power over self and situation.

Tips to shed masks and manifest authentic self:

• Turn attention inward. Most people are reactive; events and people trigger patterned responses. Catch yourself when things go wrong and do not let them push buttons.
• Discern people you communicate with. Many adults are like toddlers ruled by emotions. Everyone has different consciousness: some meet inner desires; others aggressively protect beliefs. Understanding this brings compassion.
• Set boundaries. You are in charge, yet anyone can take advantage if you allow it. Show what treatment you will not tolerate.
• Cultivate independence from opinions. Growth often comes with losses. Like rockets dropping weight, people break destructive ties. Be prepared for opposition when you become assertive and different.

Offenses people throw are about them, not you.

• Take responsibility for life. Authority within is 100% yours—you can change everything inside. You are responsible for growth, finances, emotions, and life. If you suffer ill-treatment, you choose to tolerate it and can stop by changing decision.

Becoming genuine means leaving the pattern, even when uncomfortable. Otherwise you condone living in a matrix on predecessors' paths. You have power to think and live differently. Use it.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
For many, life feels like an external force—sea that crushes boats on rocks or calms them. Such perception makes a person a toy of immense force, yet that is far from truth.

Life reflects mindset, ego beliefs, and centuries-long conditioning. People hide behind masks because authenticity risks outcast status or rejection by loved ones. Self-love and self-care signify selfishness for many, especially women expected to sacrifice careers and lives, feeling trapped by marriage and family—breeding arguments, passive aggressiveness, depression, and ruined lives.

Nonetheless, people have power to change. When most find courage to shed ego and false beliefs, they face genuine selves, meet needs and desires, and become loving and compassionate to themselves and others.

Find inner strength to manifest yourself and drop facades. Break the matrix and enjoy exceptional, harmonious life.

Try this

Practice tips to enhance awakened state:

• Sit quietly and close eyes.
• Think of dominant relationship patterns. Recall behaving like parents.
• What is pattern origin? What caused pain or anxiety? How act differently next time?
• When triggered, pause and turn attention inward.
• Act differently. It is always your choice.

Read it till the end?
